title: Alphonse Lemerre

infobox.value: (French pronunciation: [[alfɔ̃s ləmɛʁ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French"); [Canisy](/wiki/Canisy "Canisy"), [Normandy](/wiki/Normandy "Normandy"), [France](/wiki/France "France"), 1838 – [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"), France, 1912) was a 19th-century French editor and publisher, known especially for having been the first to publish many of the [Parnassian](/wiki/Parnassian "Parnassian") poets.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) Lemerre's imprints popularized the Elzévir type derived from the Roman-inspired work of [Louis Perrin](/wiki/Louis_Perrin_(printer) "Louis Perrin (printer)") from [Lyon](/wiki/Lyon "Lyon").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Suarez, F. J. F.; Woudhuysen, H. R. (2013-10-24). [*The Book: A Global History*](https://books.google.com/books?id=odDYAQAAQBAJ). OUP Oxford. p. 341. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-19-166875-3](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-19-166875-3 "Special:BookSources/978-0-19-166875-3").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

French editor, and publisher (1838–1912)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Alphonse_Lemerre_ca_1898.png/250px-Alphonse_Lemerre_ca_1898.png)](/wiki/File:Alphonse_Lemerre_ca_1898.png)

Alphonse Lemerre

**Alphonse Lemerre** (French pronunciation: [[alfɔ̃s ləmɛʁ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French"); [Canisy](/wiki/Canisy "Canisy"), [Normandy](/wiki/Normandy "Normandy"), [France](/wiki/France "France"), 1838 – [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"), France, 1912) was a 19th-century French editor and publisher, known especially for having been the first to publish many of the [Parnassian](/wiki/Parnassian "Parnassian") poets.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) Lemerre's imprints popularized the Elzévir type derived from the Roman-inspired work of [Louis Perrin](/wiki/Louis_Perrin_(printer) "Louis Perrin (printer)") from [Lyon](/wiki/Lyon "Lyon").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alphonse_Lemerre&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Marque_Alphonse_Lemerre_%28Fac_et_spera%29.jpg/250px-Marque_Alphonse_Lemerre_%28Fac_et_spera%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marque_Alphonse_Lemerre_(Fac_et_spera).jpg)

Alphonse Lemerre's publisher's mark

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Les_Hommes_N_267_Alphonse_Lemerre.jpg/250px-Les_Hommes_N_267_Alphonse_Lemerre.jpg)](/wiki/File:Les_Hommes_N_267_Alphonse_Lemerre.jpg)

Caricature of Lemerre, by [Job](/wiki/Job_(illustrator) "Job (illustrator)") (1886)

Alphonse Lemerre was the eighth child of his parents. In 1850, at age 12, he was an errand-boy in [Saint-Lô](/wiki/Saint-L%C3%B4 "Saint-Lô"). In 1860, he moved to Paris and quickly rose to prominence, becoming the "Prince de l'édition" (Prince of Publishing) and made his publisher's mark famous, which had the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") [motto](/wiki/Motto "Motto") *Fac et spera* ("*Agis et espère*" in French, "Do and Hope" in English). He opened a bookshop at 23 passage Choiseul. His shop also occupied other odd number addresses (23-33, 47).

In 1865, he began to edit Parnassian poets in [Louis-Xavier de Ricard](/wiki/Louis-Xavier_de_Ricard "Louis-Xavier de Ricard")'s revue *L'Art*, which had ten issues between November 2, 1865, and January 6, 1866. The November issue had an article by [Paul Verlaine](/wiki/Paul_Verlaine "Paul Verlaine") about [Baudelaire](/wiki/Baudelaire "Baudelaire"). In 1866, Lemerre published *[Le Parnasse contemporain](/wiki/Le_Parnasse_contemporain "Le Parnasse contemporain")*, a collection of new poetry, in 18 weekly installments from 30 March to 30 June 1866, the collected installments published in October. Two other volumes of *Le Parnasse contemporain* appeared in 1871 and 1876.

He went on to publish numerous collections. Lemerre's publications include many of the *chefs-d'œuvre* of 19th century French literature and history: [Anatole France](/wiki/Anatole_France "Anatole France"),[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) [Auguste Molinier](/wiki/Auguste_Molinier "Auguste Molinier"), [Louis Petit de Julleville](/wiki/Louis_Petit_de_Julleville "Louis Petit de Julleville"); la Petite Bibliothèque littéraire, la Bibliothèque des curieux, la Bibliothèque illustrée, la Bibliothèque dramatique, la Petite collection pour la jeunesse, les poèmes nationaux, des livres d'enseignement and other collections.

He was a mayor of [Ville d'Avray](/wiki/Ville_d%27Avray "Ville d'Avray"), republican and anticlerical in politics. He was very attached to his native Normandy. He went there often (to Canisy, to the Château de Montmirel and to [Coutainville](/wiki/Agon-Coutainville "Agon-Coutainville")) and had several properties there ([Dangy](/wiki/Dangy "Dangy"), Dais, Méterville).

In 1965, the inheritors of his estate closed the Lemerre publishing house.

## Selection of authors edited by Alphonse Lemerre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alphonse_Lemerre&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Selection of authors edited by Alphonse Lemerre")]

Alphonse Lemerre is especially known for having published [Parnassian](/wiki/Parnassian "Parnassian") poets. But he also published Classical and [Romantic](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism") authors and anthologies.

* [Théodore de Banville](/wiki/Th%C3%A9odore_de_Banville "Théodore de Banville")
* [François Coppée](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Copp%C3%A9e "François Coppée")
* [Léon Dierx](/wiki/L%C3%A9on_Dierx "Léon Dierx")
* [Théophile Gautier](/wiki/Th%C3%A9ophile_Gautier "Théophile Gautier")
* [José-Maria de Heredia](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9-Maria_de_Heredia "José-Maria de Heredia")
* [Jean Lahor](/wiki/Jean_Lahor "Jean Lahor")
* [Victor de Laprade](/wiki/Victor_de_Laprade "Victor de Laprade")
* [Charles Marie René Leconte de Lisle](/wiki/Charles_Marie_Ren%C3%A9_Leconte_de_Lisle "Charles Marie René Leconte de Lisle")
* [Catulle Mendès](/wiki/Catulle_Mend%C3%A8s "Catulle Mendès")
* [Alfred de Musset](/wiki/Alfred_de_Musset "Alfred de Musset")
* [Sully Prudhomme](/wiki/Sully_Prudhomme "Sully Prudhomme")
* [Raymond Roussel](/wiki/Raymond_Roussel "Raymond Roussel")
* [Paul Verlaine](/wiki/Paul_Verlaine "Paul Verlaine")
* [Renée Vivien](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9e_Vivien "Renée Vivien")

Collections:

* [Le Parnasse contemporain](/wiki/Le_Parnasse_contemporain "Le Parnasse contemporain"), recueil de vers nouveaux. I- 1866, II- 1869-1871, III- 1876

Anthologies:

* Anthologie des poètes français depuis les origines jusqu'à la fin du XVIIIe siècle.
* Anthologie des poètes français, XIXe siècle.
* Sonnets et eaux fortes, 1869, premier livre de peintres paru en France grâce au critique d'art Philippe Burty.
* Le Livre des Sonnets.
* Le Livre des Ballades.
